title: The Clothesline Between Buildings
text_zh: 我住的这栋楼和旁边那栋楼之间，有人拉了一条晾衣绳。我不知道是谁拉的，也不知道是什么时候拉的。但是每天都能看到绳子上挂着不同的东西：有时候是白色的床单，有时候是小孩子的衣服，有时候是一件男人的工作服。这条绳子把两栋楼连在了一起，像一座看不见的桥。有风的日子，衣服在空中飘来飘去，像在跳舞。下雨的时候，主人会赶紧把衣服收回去。但是偶尔有忘记收的时候，衣服就在雨里被淋得湿透了。第二天太阳出来，那些衣服又被重新挂了出去。这条简简单单的绳子，安安静静地记录着一个家庭每天的生活。
text_pinyin: Wǒ zhù de zhè dòng lóu hé pángbiān nà dòng lóu zhījiān, yǒu rén lā le yì tiáo liàng yī shéng. Wǒ bù zhīdào shì shéi lā de, yě bù zhīdào shì shénme shíhou lā de. Dànshì měi tiān dōu néng kàn dào shéngzi shàng guàzhe bùtóng de dōngxi: yǒu shíhou shì bái sè de chuángdān, yǒu shíhou shì xiǎo háizi de yīfu, yǒu shíhou shì yí jiàn nánrén de gōngzuòfú. Zhè tiáo shéngzi bǎ liǎng dòng lóu lián zài le yìqǐ, xiàng yí zuò kàn bu jiàn de qiáo. Yǒu fēng de rìzi, yīfu zài kōng zhōng piāo lái piāo qù, xiàng zài tiào wǔ. Xià yǔ de shíhou, zhǔrén huì gǎnjǐn bǎ yīfu shōu huíqù. Dànshì ǒu'ěr yǒu wàngjì shōu de shíhou, yīfu jiù zài yǔ lǐ bèi lín de shī tòu le. Dì èr tiān tàiyáng chūlái, nàxiē yīfu yòu bèi chóngxīn guà le chūqù. Zhè tiáo jiǎnjiǎn-dāndān de shéngzi, ān'ānjìngjìng de jìlùzhe yí gè jiātíng měi tiān de shēnghuó.
text_en: Between my building and the one next to it, someone has strung a clothesline. I don't know who put it there or when. But every day you can see different things hanging on it: sometimes white bedsheets, sometimes children's clothes, sometimes a man's work uniform. This rope connects the two buildings like an invisible bridge. On windy days, the clothes drift back and forth in the air, as if dancing. When it rains, the owner hurries to take the clothes in. But occasionally they forget, and the clothes get soaked through in the rain. The next day when the sun comes out, those clothes are hung out again. This simple rope quietly records a family's daily life.

questions.q_zh: 晾衣绳在哪里？
questions.q_en: Where is the clothesline?

questions.q_zh: 有风的时候衣服像在做什么？
questions.q_en: What do the clothes look like on windy days?

questions.q_zh: 「我」觉得这条绳子在做什么？
questions.q_en: What does 'I' think this rope does?

options.pinyin: bǎ liǎng dòng lóu lián zài yìqǐ

options.text: 记录一个家庭每天的生活
options.pinyin: jìlù yí gè jiātíng měi tiān de shēnghuó

title: The Elevator Mirror
text_zh: 我们楼的电梯里有一面大镜子。物业说装镜子是为了让电梯看起来更大一些。但是我发现，镜子的真正作用比这有趣得多。每天早上，上班的人会在镜子前面整理头发、检查衣服。有的人会对着镜子练习微笑，好像在准备面对新的一天。下午放学回来的小孩子会在镜子前面做鬼脸，然后自己笑半天。傍晚回家的人在镜子里看到自己疲倦的样子，有时候会叹一口气。同一面镜子，在不同的时间映出不同的表情。它见过每一个人最真实的样子——因为在电梯里那短短的几十秒，大部分人都觉得没有人在看，所以会放下所有的伪装。其实电梯里的镜子，是这栋楼最诚实的记录者。
text_pinyin: Wǒmen lóu de diàntī lǐ yǒu yí miàn dà jìngzi. Wùyè shuō zhuāng jìngzi shì wèile ràng diàntī kàn qǐlái gèng dà yìxiē. Dànshì wǒ fāxiàn, jìngzi de zhēnzhèng zuòyòng bǐ zhè yǒuqù de duō. Měi tiān zǎoshang, shàngbān de rén huì zài jìngzi qiánmiàn zhěnglǐ tóufa, jiǎnchá yīfu. Yǒude rén huì duìzhe jìngzi liànxí wēixiào, hǎoxiàng zài zhǔnbèi miànduì xīn de yì tiān. Xiàwǔ fàng xué huílái de xiǎo háizi huì zài jìngzi qiánmiàn zuò guǐliǎn, ránhòu zìjǐ xiào bàntiān. Bàngwǎn huí jiā de rén zài jìngzi lǐ kàn dào zìjǐ píjuàn de yàngzi, yǒu shíhou huì tàn yì kǒu qì. Tóng yí miàn jìngzi, zài bùtóng de shíjiān yìng chū bùtóng de biǎoqíng. Tā jiàn guò měi yí gè rén zuì zhēnshí de yàngzi——yīnwèi zài diàntī lǐ nà duǎnduǎn de jǐ shí miǎo, dà bùfen rén dōu juéde méiyǒu rén zài kàn, suǒyǐ huì fàng xià suǒyǒu de wěizhuāng. Qíshí diàntī lǐ de jìngzi, shì zhè dòng lóu zuì chéngshí de jìlùzhě.
text_en: In our building's elevator there's a big mirror. Property management says it was installed to make the elevator look larger. But I've found the mirror's real function is far more interesting. Every morning, commuters fix their hair and check their clothes in front of it. Some practice smiling at the mirror, as if preparing to face a new day. In the afternoon, children returning from school make funny faces in the mirror, then laugh at themselves. In the evening, people coming home see their tired faces in the mirror and sometimes sigh. The same mirror reflects different expressions at different times. It has seen everyone's most authentic self — because during those brief seconds in the elevator, most people assume nobody is watching, and they drop all pretense. Really, the elevator mirror is this building's most honest recorder.

questions.q_zh: 物业说装镜子是为了什么？
questions.q_en: What does property management say the mirror is for?

options.text: 为了让电梯看起来更大
options.pinyin: wèile ràng diàntī kàn qǐlái gèng dà
options.text_en: to make the elevator look bigger

questions.q_zh: 小孩子在镜子前面做什么？
questions.q_en: What do children do in front of the mirror?

questions.q_zh: 「我」觉得电梯镜子是什么？
questions.q_en: What does 'I' think the elevator mirror is?

options.text: 这栋楼最诚实的记录者
options.pinyin: zhè dòng lóu zuì chéngshí de jìlùzhě
yí gè méi yòng de dōngxi

title: The Man Who Feeds Pigeons
text_zh: 广场上有一个老人，每天下午三点准时来喂鸽子。他总是坐在同一张长椅上，从口袋里拿出一袋玉米粒，慢慢地撒在地上。鸽子们已经认识他了，他还没坐下来，它们就从四面八方飞过来了。有时候鸽子会飞到他的肩膀上、手上，他就那样一动不动地坐着，好像自己也变成了广场的一部分。路过的人会停下来拍照，但他从来不看镜头。有小孩子想跑过去抓鸽子的时候，他会轻轻摇头说：「别吓它们。」等玉米粒撒完了，他就安静地站起来，拍拍身上的灰，慢慢走回家。鸽子们看到他走了，还会在他后面飞一段路，好像在送他回家。
text_pinyin: Guǎngchǎng shàng yǒu yí gè lǎorén, měi tiān xiàwǔ sān diǎn zhǔnshí lái wèi gēzi. Tā zǒng shì zuò zài tóng yì zhāng chángyǐ shàng, cóng kǒudài lǐ ná chū yí dài yùmǐ lì, mànmàn de sā zài dì shàng. Gēzimen yǐjīng rènshi tā le, tā hái méi zuò xiàlái, tāmen jiù cóng sìmiàn-bāfāng fēi guòlái le. Yǒu shíhou gēzi huì fēi dào tā de jiānbǎng shàng, shǒu shàng, tā jiù nàyàng yídòng-bùdòng de zuòzhe, hǎoxiàng zìjǐ yě biàn chéng le guǎngchǎng de yí bùfen. Lùguò de rén huì tíng xiàlái pāi zhào, dàn tā cónglái bú kàn jìngtóu. Yǒu xiǎo háizi xiǎng pǎo guòqù zhuā gēzi de shíhou, tā huì qīngqīng yáo tóu shuō: 「Bié xià tāmen.」 Děng yùmǐ lì sā wán le, tā jiù ānjìng de zhàn qǐlái, pāipai shēn shàng de huī, mànmàn zǒu huí jiā. Gēzimen kàn dào tā zǒu le, hái huì zài tā hòumiàn fēi yí duàn lù, hǎoxiàng zài sòng tā huí jiā.
text_en: There's an old man on the square who comes to feed the pigeons at exactly three every afternoon. He always sits on the same bench, takes a bag of corn kernels from his pocket, and slowly scatters them on the ground. The pigeons already know him — before he even sits down, they come flying from all directions. Sometimes pigeons land on his shoulders, on his hands, and he sits perfectly still, as if he too has become part of the square. Passersby stop to take photos, but he never looks at the camera. When children try to run and catch the pigeons, he gently shakes his head and says: 'Don't scare them.' When the corn is all scattered, he quietly stands up, brushes the dust off himself, and slowly walks home. The pigeons see him go and fly behind him for a stretch, as if escorting him home.

questions.q_zh: 鸽子什么时候就飞过来了？
questions.q_en: When do the pigeons fly over?

options.pinyin: tā hái méi zuò xiàlái de shíhou

questions.q_zh: 有小孩想抓鸽子的时候他说什么？
questions.q_en: What does he say when children try to catch pigeons?

questions.q_zh: 老人走了以后鸽子怎么做？
questions.q_en: What do the pigeons do after the old man leaves?

options.text: 在他后面飞一段路，像在送他回家
options.pinyin: zài tā hòumiàn fēi yí duàn lù, xiàng zài sòng tā huí jiā
options.text_en: fly behind him for a stretch, as if escorting him home

options.pinyin: tíng zài chángyǐ shàng děng tā
options.text_en: wait for him on the bench

title: The Overpass at Rush Hour
text_zh: 每天早上八点，这座天桥上全是赶着上班的人。大家走得很快，眼睛看着手机，耳朵戴着耳机，好像每个人都活在自己的世界里。天桥很窄，只要有一个人走得慢一点，后面就会堵住。我每天都要从这座天桥走过，用的时间大概三分钟。但是这三分钟里，我能感受到整个城市的节奏。脚步声、手机铃声、远处的汽车喇叭声混在一起，组成了一首城市的交响乐。偶尔有人在天桥上停下来接电话，马上就会被后面的人推着往前走。不知道从什么时候开始，我也学会了在天桥上一边走一边看手机。只有在周末的时候，天桥才会安静下来。那时候我才发现，站在天桥上能看到很远的地方。
text_pinyin: Měi tiān zǎoshang bā diǎn, zhè zuò tiānqiáo shàng quán shì gǎnzhe shàngbān de rén. Dàjiā zǒu de hěn kuài, yǎnjing kànzhe shǒujī, ěrduo dàizhe ěrjī, hǎoxiàng měi gè rén dōu huó zài zìjǐ de shìjiè lǐ. Tiānqiáo hěn zhǎi, zhǐyào yǒu yí gè rén zǒu de màn yìdiǎn, hòumiàn jiù huì dǔ zhù. Wǒ měi tiān dōu yào cóng zhè zuò tiānqiáo zǒu guò, yòng de shíjiān dàgài sān fēnzhōng. Dànshì zhè sān fēnzhōng lǐ, wǒ néng gǎnshòu dào zhěnggè chéngshì de jiézòu. Jiǎobù shēng, shǒujī líng shēng, yuǎnchù de qìchē lǎba shēng hùn zài yìqǐ, zǔ chéng le yì shǒu chéngshì de jiāoxiǎngyuè. Ǒu'ěr yǒu rén zài tiānqiáo shàng tíng xiàlái jiē diànhuà, mǎshàng jiù huì bèi hòumiàn de rén tuīzhe wǎng qián zǒu. Bù zhīdào cóng shénme shíhou kāishǐ, wǒ yě xué huì le zài tiānqiáo shàng yìbiān zǒu yìbiān kàn shǒujī. Zhǐ yǒu zài zhōumò de shíhou, tiānqiáo cái huì ānjìng xiàlái. Nà shíhou wǒ cái fāxiàn, zhàn zài tiānqiáo shàng néng kàn dào hěn yuǎn de dìfang.
text_en: Every morning at eight, this overpass is full of people rushing to work. Everyone walks fast, eyes on their phones, earbuds in, as if each person lives in their own world. The overpass is narrow — if even one person walks a bit slow, it creates a jam behind them. I cross this overpass every day, taking about three minutes. But in those three minutes, I can feel the whole city's rhythm. Footsteps, phone ringtones, distant car horns all mix together into a city symphony. Occasionally someone stops on the overpass to take a call and is immediately pushed forward by the people behind. I don't know when I started, but I too learned to walk across the overpass while looking at my phone. Only on weekends does the overpass go quiet. That's when I discover that standing on the overpass, you can see very far away.

questions.q_zh: 天桥上的人为什么走得很快？
questions.q_en: Why do people walk fast on the overpass?

questions.q_zh: 停下来接电话的人会怎样？
questions.q_en: What happens to people who stop to take calls?

options.text: 被后面的人推着往前走
options.pinyin: bèi hòumiàn de rén tuīzhe wǎng qián zǒu

questions.q_zh: 「我」什么时候才发现天桥上能看到很远？
questions.q_en: When does 'I' discover you can see far from the overpass?

title: The Canteen Auntie
text_zh: 大学食堂有一个打饭的阿姨，同学们都叫她「手抖阿姨」——因为每次她打菜的时候，手总是会抖一下，菜就从勺子上掉回锅里了。大家都开玩笑说她是「专业减量大师」。但是有一件事很多人不知道：每到考试周，她打菜的手就不抖了，而且还会多给一点。有同学发现了这个秘密，问她为什么。她笑着说：「考试的时候你们压力大，要多吃一点才有力气考好。」后来大家才知道，她的女儿去年刚参加完高考，她特别理解学生的辛苦。从那以后，再也没有人抱怨她手抖了。大家排队到她的窗口的时候，还会跟她说：「阿姨今天辛苦了！」她每次都笑着摆摆手说：「不辛苦不辛苦。」
text_pinyin: Dàxué shítáng yǒu yí gè dǎ fàn de āyí, tóngxuémen dōu jiào tā「shǒu dǒu āyí」——yīnwèi měi cì tā dǎ cài de shíhou, shǒu zǒng shì huì dǒu yíxià, cài jiù cóng sháozi shàng diào huí guō lǐ le. Dàjiā dōu kāi wánxiào shuō tā shì「zhuānyè jiǎnliàng dàshī」. Dànshì yǒu yí jiàn shì hěn duō rén bù zhīdào: měi dào kǎoshì zhōu, tā dǎ cài de shǒu jiù bú dǒu le, érqiě hái huì duō gěi yìdiǎn. Yǒu tóngxué fāxiàn le zhège mìmì, wèn tā wèishénme. Tā xiàozhe shuō: 「Kǎoshì de shíhou nǐmen yālì dà, yào duō chī yìdiǎn cái yǒu lìqi kǎo hǎo.」 Hòulái dàjiā cái zhīdào, tā de nǚ'ér qùnián gāng cānjiā wán gāokǎo, tā tèbié lǐjiě xuéshēng de xīnkǔ. Cóng nà yǐhòu, zài yě méiyǒu rén bàoyuàn tā shǒu dǒu le. Dàjiā páiduì dào tā de chuāngkǒu de shíhou, hái huì gēn tā shuō: 「Āyí jīntiān xīnkǔ le!」 Tā měi cì dōu xiàozhe bǎibǎi shǒu shuō: 「Bù xīnkǔ bù xīnkǔ.」
text_en: In the university canteen there's a serving auntie the students all call 'Shaky Hand Auntie' — because every time she serves food, her hand trembles and some falls off the spoon back into the pot. Everyone jokes she's a 'professional portion-reducer.' But there's something most people don't know: during exam week, her hand stops shaking, and she even gives a bit extra. When a student discovered this secret and asked why, she smiled and said: 'During exams you're under a lot of pressure — you need to eat more to have energy to do well.' Later everyone learned that her daughter had just taken the college entrance exam last year, so she especially understands students' hardships. After that, nobody complained about her shaky hand again. When they line up at her window, they say: 'Auntie, you've worked hard today!' She always waves them off with a smile: 'Not hard at all.'

questions.q_zh: 同学们为什么叫她「手抖阿姨」？
questions.q_en: Why do students call her 'Shaky Hand Auntie'?

options.text: 因为打菜的时候手会抖，菜掉回锅里

options.text_en: her hand shakes and food falls back into the pot

questions.q_en: How does she change during exam week?

options.text: 手不抖了，还会多给一点
options.pinyin: shǒu bú dǒu le, hái huì duō gěi yìdiǎn
options.text_en: hand stops shaking and she gives extra

options.pinyin: zuò de cài gèng hǎochī le

questions.q_en: Why does she understand students' hardships?

options.text: 因为她女儿去年参加了高考

options.text_en: she's worked at the university a long time

title: The Footprints on the Roof
text_zh: 下了一夜的雪，早上我上天台去看雪景。但是让我吃惊的是，天台的雪地上已经有了一串脚印。脚印从天台的门走到栏杆旁边，然后又走了回来。谁会在半夜上来天台呢？我问了楼里的邻居，没有人承认是自己。保安说他半夜没有看到任何人上楼。这件事让大家讨论了好几天。有人说是小偷，有人说可能是风把雪吹出了脚印的形状。直到有一天，我遇到了住在顶楼的张奶奶。她有点不好意思地说：「是我。昨天半夜睡不着，想上来看看雪。月光照在雪上面特别好看，我站在那里看了很久。」我突然觉得，那串孤独的脚印变得温暖了起来。
text_pinyin: Xià le yí yè de xuě, zǎoshang wǒ shàng tiāntái qù kàn xuě jǐng. Dànshì ràng wǒ chījīng de shì, tiāntái de xuědì shàng yǐjīng yǒu le yí chuàn jiǎoyìn. Jiǎoyìn cóng tiāntái de mén zǒu dào lángān pángbiān, ránhòu yòu zǒu le huílái. Shéi huì zài bànyè shàng lái tiāntái ne? Wǒ wèn le lóu lǐ de línjū, méiyǒu rén chéngrèn shì zìjǐ. Bǎo'ān shuō tā bànyè méiyǒu kàn dào rènhé rén shàng lóu. Zhè jiàn shì ràng dàjiā tǎolùn le hǎo jǐ tiān. Yǒu rén shuō shì xiǎotōu, yǒu rén shuō kěnéng shì fēng bǎ xuě chuī chū le jiǎoyìn de xíngzhuàng. Zhídào yǒu yì tiān, wǒ yùdào le zhù zài dǐng lóu de Zhāng nǎinai. Tā yǒudiǎn bù hǎoyìsi de shuō: 「Shì wǒ. Zuótiān bànyè shuì bu zháo, xiǎng shàng lái kànkan xuě. Yuèguāng zhào zài xuě shàngmiàn tèbié hǎokàn, wǒ zhàn zài nàlǐ kàn le hěn jiǔ.」 Wǒ tūrán juéde, nà chuàn gūdú de jiǎoyìn biàn de wēnnuǎn le qǐlái.
text_en: It snowed all night, and in the morning I went up to the rooftop to see the snow. But to my surprise, there was already a set of footprints in the rooftop snow. The footprints went from the rooftop door to the railing and then back. Who would go up to the rooftop in the middle of the night? I asked the neighbors in the building — nobody admitted it was them. The security guard said he didn't see anyone go upstairs at night. People discussed it for days. Some said it was a thief; others said the wind might have blown the snow into footprint shapes. Until one day I ran into Grandma Zhang, who lives on the top floor. A bit embarrassed, she said: 'It was me. Last night I couldn't sleep and wanted to come up and see the snow. The moonlight on the snow was especially beautiful — I stood there looking for a long time.' Suddenly I felt those lonely footprints had become warm.

questions.q_zh: 脚印从哪里到哪里？
questions.q_en: Where did the footprints go?

title: The Handwriting
text_zh: 整理旧东西的时候，我在抽屉里发现了一叠小时候写的作文。纸已经发黄了，但是上面的字还看得清楚。那时候我的字写得又大又歪，一行字能占两行的位置。老师用红笔在旁边写了批注：「字写得再小一点就好了。」我把这些作文和现在写的字放在一起比较，简直不敢相信是同一个人写的。现在的字小了很多，也整齐了很多，但是少了一种小时候那种不管不顾的自信。小时候写字从来不怕写错，错了就涂掉重新写。现在每次下笔之前都会想半天，怕写得不够好。也许长大就是这样——我们学会了把字写得漂亮，却忘了小时候那种想写就写的勇气。
text_pinyin: Zhěnglǐ jiù dōngxi de shíhou, wǒ zài chōuti lǐ fāxiàn le yì dié xiǎo shíhou xiě de zuòwén. Zhǐ yǐjīng fā huáng le, dànshì shàngmiàn de zì hái kàn de qīngchu. Nà shíhou wǒ de zì xiě de yòu dà yòu wāi, yì háng zì néng zhàn liǎng háng de wèizhì. Lǎoshī yòng hóng bǐ zài pángbiān xiě le pīzhù: 「Zì xiě de zài xiǎo yìdiǎn jiù hǎo le.」 Wǒ bǎ zhèxiē zuòwén hé xiànzài xiě de zì fàng zài yìqǐ bǐjiào, jiǎnzhí bù gǎn xiāngxìn shì tóng yí gè rén xiě de. Xiànzài de zì xiǎo le hěn duō, yě zhěngqí le hěn duō, dànshì shǎo le yì zhǒng xiǎo shíhou nà zhǒng bùguǎn-bùgù de zìxìn. Xiǎo shíhou xiě zì cónglái bú pà xiě cuò, cuò le jiù tú diào chóngxīn xiě. Xiànzài měi cì xià bǐ zhīqián dōu huì xiǎng bàntiān, pà xiě de bú gòu hǎo. Yěxǔ zhǎng dà jiù shì zhèyàng——wǒmen xué huì le bǎ zì xiě de piàoliang, què wàng le xiǎo shíhou nà zhǒng xiǎng xiě jiù xiě de yǒngqì.
text_en: While sorting through old things, I found a stack of childhood essays in a drawer. The paper had yellowed, but the characters were still legible. Back then my handwriting was big and crooked — one line of characters could take up two lines' worth of space. The teacher wrote in red pen beside them: 'A bit smaller would be better.' I placed these essays next to my current handwriting and could hardly believe the same person wrote them. My current characters are much smaller and neater, but they've lost a certain fearless confidence from childhood. As a child I was never afraid of writing wrong — if I made a mistake, I'd cross it out and write again. Now I deliberate for ages before putting pen to paper, afraid it won't be good enough. Maybe growing up is like this: we learn to write beautifully, but forget the courage we had as children to just write.

questions.q_zh: 小时候的字是什么样的？
questions.q_en: What was the childhood handwriting like?

questions.q_zh: 老师在旁边写了什么？
questions.q_en: What did the teacher write beside the essay?

options.text: 字写得再小一点就好了
options.pinyin: zì xiě de zài xiǎo yìdiǎn jiù hǎo le
options.text_en: a bit smaller would be better

questions.q_zh: 「我」觉得长大以后少了什么？
questions.q_en: What does 'I' think is lost in growing up?

options.pinyin: xiǎng xiě jiù xiě de yǒngqì

title: The Quiet After Rain
text_zh: 刚下完雨的城市有一种特别的安静。空气被洗过一样干净，能闻到泥土和树叶的味道。地上到处都是水洼，像一面面小镜子，映着天空和楼房。有几只鸟飞回来了，停在电线上叫，声音特别清脆。路上的车比刚才少了很多，只有几辆出租车慢慢地开过去，轮胎压过水洼的声音很好听。便利店门口有一个小孩子专门在踩水洼玩，每踩一下水就溅起来，他笑得很开心。他妈妈在旁边一边叹气一边笑。我站在路边等公交车，看着这一切，觉得这就是城市最真实的样子——不是白天的忙碌，不是夜晚的灯光，而是雨后那几分钟的安静和干净。可惜这种感觉很快就会消失，等太阳出来，一切又会恢复到以前的样子。
text_pinyin: Gāng xià wán yǔ de chéngshì yǒu yì zhǒng tèbié de ānjìng. Kōngqì bèi xǐ guò yíyàng gānjìng, néng wén dào nítǔ hé shùyè de wèidao. Dì shàng dàochù dōu shì shuǐwā, xiàng yí miàn miàn xiǎo jìngzi, yìngzhe tiānkōng hé lóufáng. Yǒu jǐ zhī niǎo fēi huílái le, tíng zài diànxiàn shàng jiào, shēngyīn tèbié qīngcuì. Lù shàng de chē bǐ gāngcái shǎo le hěn duō, zhǐ yǒu jǐ liàng chūzūchē mànmàn de kāi guòqù, lúntāi yā guò shuǐwā de shēngyīn hěn hǎotīng. Biànlì diàn ménkǒu yǒu yí gè xiǎo háizi zhuānmén zài cǎi shuǐwā wán, měi cǎi yíxià shuǐ jiù jiàn qǐlái, tā xiào de hěn kāixīn. Tā māma zài pángbiān yìbiān tàn qì yìbiān xiào. Wǒ zhàn zài lù biān děng gōngjiāo chē, kànzhe zhè yíqiè, juéde zhè jiù shì chéngshì zuì zhēnshí de yàngzi——búshì báitiān de mánglù, búshì yèwǎn de dēngguāng, érshì yǔ hòu nà jǐ fēnzhōng de ānjìng hé gānjìng. Kěxī zhè zhǒng gǎnjué hěn kuài jiù huì xiāoshī, děng tàiyáng chūlái, yíqiè yòu huì huīfù dào yǐqián de yàngzi.
text_en: A city right after rain has a special kind of quiet. The air is as clean as if it's been washed — you can smell the earth and leaves. Puddles everywhere on the ground, like little mirrors reflecting the sky and buildings. A few birds have flown back, perching on power lines and calling out in crisp voices. There are far fewer cars on the road now — just a few taxis driving slowly past, the sound of their tires over puddles quite pleasant. In front of a convenience store, a child deliberately stomps in puddles, water splashing up with each step, laughing happily. His mother stands beside him, sighing and smiling at the same time. I stand at the roadside waiting for the bus, watching all of this, feeling that this is the city's most authentic self — not the busyness of daytime, not the lights of night, but the quiet and cleanliness of those few minutes after rain. It's a pity this feeling will disappear quickly — once the sun comes out, everything will return to how it was.

questions.q_en: What is the air like after rain?

title: The Tea Thermos
text_zh: 爸爸有一个用了十几年的旧保温杯，银色的外壳已经被磨得到处都是划痕。妈妈买了好几次新杯子给他，他都不用，还是用那个旧的。每天早上他都会泡一杯浓茶放进去，然后带着它去上班。中午的时候再泡一次，喝到晚上下班。他说这个杯子是刚工作的时候一个老同事送给他的，那个同事后来去了别的城市，他们已经很多年没有联系了。但是每天用这个杯子喝茶的时候，他就会想起那个人。我问爸爸为什么不联系那个同事，他说：「有些人不用联系也能记住。只要我还在用这个杯子，就好像他还在我身边一样。」也许对爸爸来说，这个旧保温杯已经不只是一个杯子了，它是一段友情的容器。
text_pinyin: Bàba yǒu yí gè yòng le shí jǐ nián de jiù bǎowēn bēi, yín sè de wàiké yǐjīng bèi mó de dàochù dōu shì huáhén. Māma mǎi le hǎo jǐ cì xīn bēizi gěi tā, tā dōu bú yòng, háishi yòng nàge jiù de. Měi tiān zǎoshang tā dōu huì pào yì bēi nóng chá fàng jìnqù, ránhòu dàizhe tā qù shàngbān. Zhōngwǔ de shíhou zài pào yí cì, hē dào wǎnshang xiàbān. Tā shuō zhège bēizi shì gāng gōngzuò de shíhou yí gè lǎo tóngshì sòng gěi tā de, nàge tóngshì hòulái qù le biéde chéngshì, tāmen yǐjīng hěn duō nián méiyǒu liánxì le. Dànshì měi tiān yòng zhège bēizi hē chá de shíhou, tā jiù huì xiǎng qǐ nàge rén. Wǒ wèn bàba wèishénme bù liánxì nàge tóngshì, tā shuō: 「Yǒuxiē rén búyòng liánxì yě néng jìzhù. Zhǐyào wǒ hái zài yòng zhège bēizi, jiù hǎoxiàng tā hái zài wǒ shēnbiān yíyàng.」 Yěxǔ duì bàba lái shuō, zhège jiù bǎowēn bēi yǐjīng bù zhǐ shì yí gè bēizi le, tā shì yí duàn yǒuqíng de róngqì.
text_en: My dad has an old thermos he's used for over a decade. The silver shell is covered in scratches from wear. Mom has bought him new cups many times, but he never uses them — still the old one. Every morning he brews a cup of strong tea, puts it in the thermos, and takes it to work. At noon he brews another cup, drinking through to the end of the workday. He says this cup was given to him by an old colleague when he first started working. That colleague later moved to another city, and they haven't been in touch for many years. But every day when he drinks tea from this cup, he thinks of that person. I asked Dad why he doesn't contact the colleague. He said: 'Some people you can remember without being in touch. As long as I'm still using this cup, it's as if he's still by my side.' Maybe for Dad, this old thermos is no longer just a cup — it's a vessel holding a friendship.

title: The GPS Argument
text_zh: 我爸开车的时候有一个习惯——喜欢跟导航吵架。导航说「请在前方路口右转」，他偏要直走，然后导航就会说「正在为您重新规划路线」。每次听到这句话，他就会不高兴地说：「我在这个城市住了三十年了，还需要你告诉我怎么走？」但是他自己选的路经常会堵车，最后还是得听导航的。妈妈坐在旁边，每次都忍不住笑。有一次我们全家出去旅游，在一个完全不认识的城市里，爸爸终于安安静静地听了导航的话。但是到了目的地以后，他还是说了一句：「其实我刚才想到了一条更近的路。」全家人都笑了。妈妈说：「你爸不是跟导航吵架，他是不愿意承认自己不认路。」
text_pinyin: Wǒ bà kāi chē de shíhou yǒu yí gè xíguàn——xǐhuan gēn dǎoháng chǎo jià. Dǎoháng shuō「qǐng zài qiánfāng lùkǒu yòu zhuǎn」, tā piān yào zhí zǒu, ránhòu dǎoháng jiù huì shuō「zhèngzài wèi nín chóngxīn guīhuà lùxiàn」. Měi cì tīng dào zhè jù huà, tā jiù huì bù gāoxìng de shuō: 「Wǒ zài zhège chéngshì zhù le sānshí nián le, hái xūyào nǐ gàosu wǒ zěnme zǒu?」 Dànshì tā zìjǐ xuǎn de lù jīngcháng huì dǔ chē, zuìhòu háishi děi tīng dǎoháng de. Māma zuò zài pángbiān, měi cì dōu rěn bu zhù xiào. Yǒu yí cì wǒmen quán jiā chūqù lǚyóu, zài yí gè wánquán bù rènshi de chéngshì lǐ, bàba zhōngyú ān'ānjìngjìng de tīng le dǎoháng de huà. Dànshì dào le mùdìdì yǐhòu, tā háishi shuō le yí jù: 「Qíshí wǒ gāngcái xiǎng dào le yì tiáo gèng jìn de lù.」 Quán jiā rén dōu xiào le. Māma shuō: 「Nǐ bà búshì gēn dǎoháng chǎo jià, tā shì bú yuànyì chéngrèn zìjǐ bú rèn lù.」
text_en: My dad has a habit when driving — he likes to argue with the GPS. When the GPS says 'please turn right at the next intersection,' he insists on going straight, and then the GPS says 'recalculating your route.' Every time he hears that, he says unhappily: 'I've lived in this city for thirty years — do I still need you to tell me where to go?' But the routes he chooses often get stuck in traffic, so he ends up following the GPS anyway. Mom sits beside him, unable to keep from laughing every time. Once our whole family went on a trip to a city we'd never been to, and Dad finally listened quietly to the GPS. But after arriving at the destination, he still said: 'Actually, I just thought of a shorter route.' The whole family laughed. Mom said: 'Your dad isn't arguing with the GPS — he just doesn't want to admit he doesn't know the way.'

questions.q_en: What does Dad say after hearing 'recalculating route'?
